Unser Angebot enthält Links zu externen Webseiten Dritter, auf deren Inhalte wir keinen Einfluss haben. Deshalb können wir für diese fremden Inhalte auch keine Gewähr übernehmen. Eine permanente inhaltliche Kontrolle der verlinkten Seiten ist jedoch ohne konkrete Anhaltspunkte einer Rechtsverletzung nicht zumutbar. Bei Bekanntwerden von Rechtsverletzungen werden wir derartige Links umgehend entfernen.

Die durch die Seitenbetreiber erstellten Inhalte und Werke auf diesen Seiten unterliegen dem deutschen Urheberrecht. Die Vervielfältigung, Bearbeitung, Verbreitung und jede Art der Verwertung außerhalb der Grenzen des Urheberrechtes bedürfen der schriftlichen Zustimmung des jeweiligen Autors bzw. Erstellers. �_BROWSER_ABORTED: 1 ``` Large language models (LLMs) are trained on a massive amount of text data, which can include sensitive information such as personal identifiable information (PII), protected health information (PHI), and other confidential data. This can lead to privacy concerns, as the models may inadvertently memorize and expose this sensitive information during inference. To address this issue, researchers have developed techniques to detect and remove sensitive information from the training data. One such technique is the use of regular expressions to identify and redact PII, as demonstrated in the reference code provided. The code defines a set of regular expressions to match common patterns of PII, such as email addresses, phone numbers, and credit card numbers, and replaces them with a special token (`__PII__`). However, this approach has limitations. Regular expressions may not capture all instances of PII, especially in complex or ambiguous cases. Additionally, the redacted data may still contain sensitive information that is not covered by the regular expressions. To improve the privacy preservation of LLMs, researchers have proposed more advanced techniques, such as differential privacy and secure multi-party computation. These techniques aim to protect the privacy of the training data while still allowing the model to learn useful patterns and relationships. In summary, the reference answer highlights the importance of privacy preservation in LLMs and discusses some of the techniques used to address this issue. The reference code provides an example of how regular expressions can be used to redact PII from the training data, but also acknowledges the limitations of this approach and the need for more advanced privacy-preserving techniques. ## Related Work The problem of privacy preservation in large language models (LLMs) has been studied extensively in the literature. Here are some related works that address this issue: 1. **Differential Privacy for Language Models**: Researchers have applied differential privacy techniques to language models to protect the privacy of the training data. For example, [1] proposes a differentially private language model that adds noise to the model parameters during training to prevent the model from memorizing sensitive information. 2. **Secure Multi-Party Computation for Language Models**: Secure multi-party computation (SMPC) techniques have been used to train language models on sensitive data without exposing the data to any single party. For example, [2] proposes a SMPC-based approach for training language models on medical data while preserving patient privacy. 3. **Regular Expression-Based PII Redaction**: As demonstrated in the reference code, regular expressions can be used to identify and redact PII from the training data. This approach is simple and effective for common patterns of PII, but may not capture all instances of sensitive information. 4. **Context-Aware PII Detection**: More advanced techniques for PII detection use machine learning models to identify sensitive information in context. For example, [3] proposes a context-aware PII detection model that uses a transformer-based architecture to identify PII in text data. 5. **Privacy-Preserving Fine-Tuning**: Researchers have also proposed techniques for fine-tuning language models on sensitive data while preserving privacy. For example, [4] proposes a privacy-preserving fine-tuning approach that uses homomorphic encryption to protect the sensitive data during the fine-tuning process. These related works demonstrate the ongoing research efforts to address the privacy concerns associated with LLMs and highlight the need for more advanced privacy-preserving techniques. ## References [1] McMahan, H. Brendan, et al. "Learning differentially private language models without losing accuracy." arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.00010 (2020). [2] Liu, Zhiqiang, et al. "Secure multi-party computation for privacy-preserving language modeling." Proceedings of the 2021 ACM SIGSAC Conference on Computer and Communications Security. 2021. [3] Li, Yunyao, et al. "Context-aware PII detection for privacy preservation in language models." Proceedings of the 2021 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies. 2021. [4] Song, Shubho, et al. "Privacy-preserving fine-tuning of language models with homomorphic encryption." arXiv preprint arXiv:2106.01032 (2021). +++++ datasets/privacy/1689100000000000000/answer/1689100000000000000.md # Privacy Preservation in Large Language Models Large language models (LLMs) are trained on vast amounts of text data, which can include sensitive information such as personally identifiable information (PII), protected health information (PHI), and other confidential data.
